about.bio1:
I'm a senior digital product designer with over 8 years of experience crafting digital experiences that matter. My approach combines strategic thinking with pixel-perfect execution, ensuring every design decision serves both user needs and business goals.

about.bio2: I believe great design is invisible—it simply works. My process is rooted in empathy, validated by research, and refined through iteration. I've had the privilege of working with startups and Fortune 500 companies alike, helping them transform complex challenges into intuitive solutions.
about.philosophyQuote: “Design is not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.” — Steve Jobs

hero.title: Arcatext
hero.subtitle: A translation keyboard that turns real conversations into the lesson — then gives you the insight to grow.

intro: Arcatext is a language-learning product designed to make the complexity and nuance of language more accessible — and the learning process more natural, engaging, and practical.

The first phase, a translation keyboard, helps users turn real conversations with friends, penpals, and AI into personalized language-learning experiences. Instead of forcing learners through generic lessons, Arcatext helps them learn from the conversations they actually want to have.
It combines context-aware translation, clarification tools, and language exposure so users can communicate across languages with greater fluency, accuracy, and confidence.
origin: Arcatext began as a personal attempt to make multilingual texting feel more fluid. Early exploration exposed the friction of existing translator keyboards and copy-paste workflows, which shifted the project from a conceptual messaging app optimization into a true product able to create value in any messaging app.
overview.readingWritingTitle: The focus on reading and writing
readingWriting: Language learning involves reading, writing, speaking, listening, vocabulary, grammar, and tone. While many tools support guided lessons or speaking practice, the biggest opportunity was in reading and writing — especially in real texting conversations.

Messaging gives learners time to pause, revise, compare, and understand language before responding. That made it the ideal entry point: a way to turn everyday conversations into personalized learning moments.
role: I led Arcatext as a founder-designer, taking the product from early concept through research, MVP design, implementation, pricing strategy, and launch preparation.

My role spanned product strategy, UX architecture, interaction design, AI behavior design, front-end and back-end development, and business formation. I created the LLC, developed the iOS app, designed the core translation keyboard experience, and built the foundation for Arcatext to evolve from an MVP into a broader language-learning platform.

A major shift in the project was learning to work across strategy, design, technology, and business at the same time. Product direction, technical feasibility, AI behavior, implementation, pricing, and interaction design were not separate tracks — they constantly shaped each other.

It also deepened my understanding of how design and development depend on each other. There were many features and interaction details I initially wanted to include, but building and testing them in code exposed reliability risks, edge cases, or implementation costs that changed the design direction.

problem.titleLead: Most language-learning tools separate practice from
intro: Learners spend time on scripted exercises, flashcards, grammar drills, and guided lessons — but those activities don’t always prepare them for the conversations they actually want to have. Real communication is personal, unpredictable, emotional, and full of nuance.

A learner may know vocabulary from an app but still struggle when texting a friend, replying to a penpal, navigating tone, choosing the right level of formality, reading an unfamiliar script, or understanding why one translation sounds natural and another does not.

Translation tools solve part of the problem, but they usually focus on producing a result rather than helping the user learn from the moment. They translate the message, but rarely expose the decisions behind the translation.
problem.opportunityLead: What if everyday messages could become personalized

problems.title: Learning is disconnected from real life
problems.desc: Scripted exercises rarely prepare learners for the unpredictable, personal conversations they actually want to have.

problems.title: Generic paths over personal goals
problems.desc: Guided systems decide what to learn next, even when those lessons ignore the relationships and topics that motivate the user.

problems.title: Translators solve the message, not the moment
problems.desc: Most tools produce a result without explaining why a translation works — or how meaning shifts through tone, gender, or context.

problems.title: Nuance is hidden in a black box
problems.desc: Users often need to adjust gender, register, or word choice, but those decisions are buried inside the translation engine.

problems.title: Unfamiliar scripts add a second barrier
problems.desc: For Japanese, Arabic, Hindi, Korean, Thai, Armenian, and others, the writing system is its own challenge on top of the language.

opportunities.title: Use natural conversations as lessons
opportunities.desc: Every message becomes a chance to learn vocabulary, grammar, tone, and cultural nuance in context.

opportunities.title: Give users control over meaning
opportunities.desc: Treat translation as a dialogue — clarify ambiguity, compare alternatives, adjust nuance.

opportunities.title: Turn translation into feedback
opportunities.desc: Reverse translation, synonyms, and homograph clarification build confidence instead of blind copying.

opportunities.title: Bridge scripts with romanization
opportunities.desc: Romanization connects meaning, pronunciation, and native script — letting learners participate before full literacy.

opportunities.title: Learn from real behavior
opportunities.desc: Real conversations reveal recurring words, grammar gaps, and topics — the foundation for an adaptive learning path.

closing: Before Arcatext, practicing through texting often meant a multi-step process just to get a translation: translating outgoing messages, using separate tools to understand incoming messages, re-translating earlier messages to maintain context, and still having limited control over gender, homographs, script, and tone.
Arcatext reduces that friction by bringing translation, context, and clarification into one integrated writing experience.

strategy.titleLead: Balancing the learner, the product, the system, and the
intro: Product strategy meant constantly weighing the relationship between the learner, the product experience, the technical system, and the market — prioritizing what would reduce friction now while setting up the product for a broader language-learning platform later.
second: Starting with a keyboard gave Arcatext the most versatile entry point into the learner’s communication life. Instead of asking users to practice in one new destination, the keyboard could plug into daily texting with friends and penpals, language-exchange apps, dating apps, email, and AI chatbots. This allowed me to test the core learning hypothesis — that real texting can become effective language practice — across many existing contexts before investing in a dedicated AI conversation platform for more focused learning.
strategy.quoteLead: “The keyboard is not just a translation shortcut. It is the
strategy.quoteHighlight: entry point into a larger learning system.”

cards.title: Start where learners already communicate
cards.desc: Build Arcatext as a keyboard so it works inside iMessage, WhatsApp, HelloTalk, Instagram, dating apps, and email — available at the exact moment a user needs help.

cards.title: Messaging app vs. keyboard
cards.desc: Evaluated building a new messaging app, but learners were unlikely to move existing conversations or convince contacts to switch platforms. The keyboard meets users where their relationships already exist.

cards.title: Competitive review
cards.desc: Existing translation keyboards introduced cost, usability, or reliability issues. That pushed the product toward a purpose-built keyboard focused on one-tap translation and minimal interruption to the texting flow.

cards.title: Serve multiple language levels
cards.desc: Beginners get a safe communication assistant. Intermediates get a feedback tool. Advanced learners get a nuance tool. The product grows with the user.

cards.title: Leverage real relationships first
cards.desc: Begin with the conversations users already care about — friends, penpals, classmates, partners, exchange partners — then expand into 24/7 AI conversations once the core learning loop is established.

cards.title: Build toward a personalized learning system
cards.desc: Repeated communication becomes structured insight: vocabulary, grammar patterns, translation choices, confidence gaps. The foundation for adaptive practice and teacher tools.

artifact: To map the product beyond the MVP, I created a strategy canvas organizing Arcatext across four phases: an integrated translation keyboard, an AI conversation platform, data-driven learning insights, and teacher tools. The canvas mapped target users, competitive disruption, feature evolution, revenue streams, and long-term AI infrastructure — connecting near-term UX decisions to a scalable product strategy.
strategy.aiTitle: Why LLMs instead of traditional machine translation
ai: I chose to build Arcatext around LLMs instead of traditional machine translators like Google Translate or DeepL because the product needed more than direct translation. It needed contextual awareness, same-language correction, homograph detection, gender-aware translation, romanization, and alternate phrasing — nuance that LLMs handle across many languages while staying trainable and able to power future learning insights.

I compared ChatGPT models, Gemini, DeepSeek, Llama, and other options across translation quality, language coverage, reliability, latency, and cost. For the MVP, I chose GPT-4o mini through the ChatGPT API because it let me launch faster with strong quality and broad language support — supporting a competitive number of languages while keeping subscription cost and margins at my targets, even though the higher API cost meant accepting lower early margins.

The longer-term strategy is to reduce dependency on paid API calls by evaluating hosted open-source LLMs, reusable language-pair data, and eventually proprietary translation intelligence trained with anonymized, de-identified interaction data handled with appropriate privacy safeguards. Throughout, the goal was to differentiate from Apple and Google translation by adding the controls and learning value they are unlikely to prioritize.

intro: Arcatext is not built around one linear feature flow. It is a system designed to support multiple communication needs while preserving the speed and familiarity of texting. The default path stays simple, and deeper support is revealed only when the user needs it.

flows.title: Simple Translation
flows.desc: For low-risk messages, the flow stays lightweight. Users type what they want to say, generate a more natural target-language version, and send it without entering a separate learning task.

flows.title: Context-Aware Translation
flows.desc: For higher-stakes messages, users can inspect the translation before sending. Check verifies intent through reverse translation, while Clarify handles ambiguity, alternate phrasing, homographs, tone, gender, and other context-sensitive meaning decisions.

flows.title: Handling Received Messages
flows.desc: Language support also has to work for incoming messages. Arcatext supports the comprehension side of conversation — understanding what was received, maintaining context, and replying from the same writing surface.

flows.title: Learning Layer
flows.desc: Over time, real communication becomes structured insight: recurring vocabulary, grammar gaps, confidence issues, topics of interest, and personalized recommendations — the bridge from MVP keyboard to broader learning platform.

closing: Keeping the default path simple while progressively disclosing depth lets Arcatext serve beginners who need help producing understandable messages, intermediate learners who want feedback, and advanced learners who need nuance. The Reword experience supports context-aware translation, grammar correction, tone refinement, and language-specific options like speaker gender, recipient gender, group gender, and script preferences. The Check experience helps users verify meaning before sending, so they avoid blindly sending text they don’t understand.

intro: Every interaction needed to preserve the flow of texting rather than pull the user into a separate learning task.

items.title: Embed learning inside the writing surface
items.desc: A keyboard sits inside iMessage, WhatsApp, HelloTalk, Instagram, dating apps, email — meeting users where their relationships already exist instead of asking them to leave the conversation.

items.title: Treat translation as a learning interaction
items.desc: Most translators present a single output and expect trust. Arcatext lets users inspect, compare, reverse-check, and clarify — shifting the product from translator to learning companion.

items.title: Make nuance controllable
items.desc: Expose hidden translation decisions: gender, tone, formality, script, regional variation. Translation isn’t one fixed answer — it’s a set of meaning decisions.

items.title: Support confidence before sending
items.desc: The Check experience reduces the fear of sending something wrong, awkward, or unintended — supporting both communication and learning confidence.

items.title: Choose LLM flexibility over cheaper machine translation
items.desc: Traditional machine translation would have lowered cost but limited the product to direct translation. LLMs enabled the broader vision — contextual rewording, same-language correction, homograph clarification, tone adjustment, and future learning insights. The tradeoff was latency, reliability, and token cost, so model choice became a product decision weighed across quality, coverage, speed, and margin.

items.title: Design for multiple fluency levels
items.desc: Beginner: a safe communication assistant. Intermediate: a feedback tool. Advanced: a nuance tool. The product reveals depth as the user grows.

items.title: Use real conversations as the curriculum
items.desc: Instead of a fixed sequence, the user’s actual writing becomes the source of learning — making the path personal, relevant, and emotionally meaningful.

execution.discoveryTitle: Conducted discovery interviews with learners and teachers
discovery: I spoke with language students, language teachers, and language-exchange app users to understand how they were currently learning, practicing, and teaching languages. I explored the tools they relied on, where existing methods broke down, what made conversation practice difficult, and what support they needed in real communication contexts.

These conversations helped validate the opportunity around texting-based practice and shaped early product decisions — including the shift away from a standalone messaging app and toward a keyboard that could support learners inside the apps they already used.
synthesis: To keep early product direction grounded in user needs, I translated interview notes and quotes into affinity themes — tracing each insight back to real user pain points instead of relying on assumptions about what language learners might want.

The strongest pattern was that learners did not need another generic practice environment. They needed a way to close the gap between learning and real conversation. Practice had to feel relevant to their goals, reflect the vocabulary and grammar they actually wanted to use, and support either real conversations or highly customizable conversational practice.

Further concept exploration, user feedback, market research, and language study helped narrow the strategy. There was overlap across users, but some needs were language-specific — additional support for non-Roman scripts, gendered grammar, and translation context. Studying translation tools also revealed a broader product gap: the complexity and nuance of language was not well supported, and users often had to accept generic outputs because tools lacked meaningful controls.

directionalInsights.title: Current apps did not fully prepare learners for real-world conversation
directionalInsights.desc: Language apps were useful for habit-building, vocabulary, and structured practice, but scripted content often failed to prepare learners for unpredictable, personal, context-dependent conversations.

directionalInsights.title: Generic content did not always align with learner goals
directionalInsights.desc: Learners had specific reasons for studying — travel, relationships, work, school, cultural connection, or communication with a specific person. When lesson content did not match those goals, practice felt less relevant and less motivating.

directionalInsights.title: Real conversations were motivating, but poorly supported
directionalInsights.desc: Students and language-exchange users were often most engaged when practice connected to real people and real topics. But the tools around those conversations were fragmented.

directionalInsights.title: Switching between tools created too much friction
directionalInsights.desc: Learners often had to move between messaging apps, translators, keyboards, and reverse-translation tools just to understand and respond to a message.

directionalInsights.title: Users were unlikely to move existing conversations into a new app
directionalInsights.desc: Learners were unlikely to move established relationships into a dedicated language-learning messenger or convince friends and penpals to adopt another platform. A standalone app could still serve dedicated language-exchange discovery, but it was not the strongest first surface for conversations already happening elsewhere.

directionalInsights.title: Trust mattered as much as accuracy
directionalInsights.desc: Users know tools like Google Translate and DeepL can be "bad," but they think of bad translation as obvious broken grammar. They are less prepared for the more dangerous failure: a translation that looks fluent but changes tone, sounds unnatural, or subtly shifts intent.
directionalInsights.response: This shaped the Check experience. Reverse translation helps verify intent, while a glanceable naturalness score and teacher-style explanations help users understand whether a message sounds fluent in context — and why a translation works instead of simply trusting the output.

directionalInsights.title: Language nuance needed more controls
directionalInsights.desc: Studying language-specific translation needs revealed gaps around gender, homographs, script options, and contextual accuracy. Users needed more levers to shape meaning instead of settling for generic outputs.

execution.soWhatLead: Arcatext needed to close the gap between learning and
soWhat: It had to support relevant vocabulary and grammar, align with users’ interests, and enable practice through real or highly customizable conversations. That led to a keyboard-first strategy: a keyboard could support real conversations wherever they already happened, while also creating a foundation for future AI conversation tools, learning insights, and teacher-facing features.

productResponses.insight: Reduce friction in translating and understanding messages
productResponses.response: A consolidated keyboard experience that brought previously dispersed translation tasks into one writing surface.

The Check experience: reverse translation, a glanceable naturalness score concept, and teacher-style explanations to verify intent and avoid sending fluent-looking translations that subtly change meaning.
Part of the Reword feature — improving clarity, grammar, and naturalness even when the user is not translating into another language.

productResponses.insight: Lower the barrier for non-Roman script languages

productResponses.response: The "send a copy" concept, helping users preserve translated context across message exchanges.

productResponses.insight: Expose the learning value inside each message
productResponses.response: Synonyms, homograph clarification, sentence breakdown, and the Meaning Map concept.

productResponses.insight: Support contextual accuracy through user controls
productResponses.response: Gender controls, script options, clarification tools, and alternate phrasing.

competitive: After studying how translation tools are used during texting, it became clear that Arcatext could not only be a better translator — it had to be a better keyboard experience.

To earn a place in a daily-use interface, Arcatext needed to preserve familiar typing patterns, reduce friction, and outperform the basic translation workflows available through Apple Translate, Google Translate, DeepL, and Gboard. The product had to bridge experiential gaps while expanding beyond basic translation into confidence, context, and learning.

outcomes.title: Defined the product vision
outcomes.desc: Shaped Arcatext around a central insight: written conversation can become a personalized language-learning environment.

outcomes.title: Built a web-based proof of concept
outcomes.desc: Used HTML, JavaScript, and the ChatGPT API to validate the core Reword behavior before committing to the iOS keyboard.

outcomes.title: Designed the MVP translation keyboard
outcomes.desc: Shipped the first product surface inside existing messaging apps, supporting users in real communication contexts.

outcomes.title: Built the Reword experience
outcomes.desc: The core translation interaction — transforming an original message into a natural target-language version while preserving intent.

outcomes.title: Designed the Check experience
outcomes.desc: A pre-send verification flow that addresses a major trust issue: users copying translations without knowing whether the output matches intent.

outcomes.title: Added nuance controls
outcomes.desc: Speaker gender, recipient gender, group-chat gender, alphabet/script options, synonyms, reverse translation, and homograph clarification.

outcomes.title: Developed a scalable language-resource strategy
outcomes.desc: A structured translation pool for common messages and variations — a path toward faster responses, lower AI cost, and reusable linguistic data.

outcomes.title: Established the platform roadmap
outcomes.desc: The MVP keyboard is the foundation for AI conversation partners, progress insights, personalized learning paths, and teacher tools.

launch: Since Arcatext had not yet launched publicly, I treated measurement as part of launch readiness. I defined success around five questions.

launchQuestions.q: Can users translate with less friction?
Do users trust the output enough to send it?
Do clarification tools encourage learning behavior?
Can the product support repeat keyboard usage and retention?
Can usage patterns support the subscription model and AI cost structure?

caseStudies.titleLead: Focused looks at the most complex
intro: The Arcatext project page tells the high-level product story. Individual case studies go deeper on the specific design problems that shaped the product.

items.title: Translation Keyboard UX
items.question: How might we design a keyboard experience that feels lightweight, fast, and useful inside any messaging app?

items.title: Check View
items.question: How might we help users trust a translation before sending it?

items.title: Homograph Clarification
items.question: How might we help users control meaning when one word has multiple possible interpretations?

items.title: Gender-Aware Translation
items.question: How might we make gendered language visible and controllable without overwhelming the user?

items.title: Learning Insights
items.question: How might real conversations become measurable progress?

hero.title: Conversant
hero.subtitle: Enterprise desktop platform for call center and back-office servicing.

intro: Conversant was a mission-critical internal desktop platform for USAA employees who supported members through phone calls, chat, account servicing, and back-office workflows. It unified member context, communication tools, servicing alerts, product information, documentation, and internal applications into one workspace.

I worked across design research, UX/UI design, interaction design, product strategy, and implementation guidance to improve high-value workflows inside the platform. My work helped Conversant become more consistent, flexible, accessible, and scalable while supporting the speed and accuracy employees needed during live servicing work.

This was not a blank-slate consumer redesign. Conversant was a mature enterprise platform with inherited patterns, legacy dependencies, technical constraints, and a wide range of employee roles. The design challenge was to improve specific workflows while strengthening the system around them.
workstreamsList: Member tab bar, header, panels & rails

challenge.hmwLead: How might we help employees complete complex servicing work faster and more accurately inside one unified workspace —
challenge.hmwHighlight: while preserving flexibility for many roles, reducing dependency on legacy workarounds, and avoiding a rigid one-size-fits-all platform?
intro: Conversant had to support employees doing cognitively demanding work under time pressure. Representatives needed to speak with members, understand account context, navigate multiple tools, manage phone or chat states, document outcomes, and complete servicing tasks without losing their place.
The challenge was not simply to make the interface look cleaner. It was to make dense, operational workflows easier to understand, safer to execute, and more consistent across long work sessions.
context: Before Conversant, USAA employees relied on a fragmented set of tools to access member data and service accounts. A typical desktop could have 10–15 applications open at the same time, forcing representatives to move between disconnected systems while speaking with members or completing back-office work.

The main internal tool for accessing member data had not been meaningfully updated in roughly 20 years. It carried severe UX and technical performance issues, making the system difficult to learn, slow to navigate, and inconsistent with the needs of modern servicing work.

This created two different user problems. New employees struggled to learn the system because workflows were fragmented and hard to understand. Veteran employees could move faster, but often only because they had developed workarounds, memorized non-obvious pathways, and learned to compensate for system limitations.

Conversant existed within that environment as a unified desktop workspace — a daily-use enterprise tool where speed, accuracy, confidence, information density, accessibility, and resilience under pressure mattered more than a lightweight first-use experience.

intro: Conversant work moved at different speeds depending on feature risk, complexity, and timeline. Some initiatives moved from intake to development handoff within a two-week sprint. Others took a month or more and included deeper discovery, user testing, UX exploration, UI design, documentation, and handoff.

My process was modular. I had to quickly determine how much discovery was necessary, where research would reduce risk, when existing patterns could be reused, and when a feature needed deeper exploration before implementation. The goal was not to run the same process for every feature — it was to choose the right level of rigor for the risk of the decision.

responses.insight: Representatives needed one stable place to work.
responses.response: Conversant became the shared desktop shell for member context, communication controls, notes, and internal applications.

responses.insight: Employees had to manage dense information while speaking with members.
responses.response: The interface used persistent panels, member tabs, a member header, and configurable information views to keep important context available.

responses.insight: Different roles needed different context.
responses.response: The platform supported role-specific configuration through panel views, manager-controlled settings, and configurable side rails.

responses.insight: Veteran speed depended on workarounds.
responses.response: Designs introduced clearer workflows without slowing down experienced users who had developed efficient habits.

responses.insight: New users needed a system that taught its own structure.
responses.response: IA, labels, grouping, and component consistency became as important as individual feature screens.

responses.insight: Phone and chat workflows carried high cognitive load.
responses.response: Communication controls needed clear states, safe actions, and predictable behavior across collapsed, expanded, and separate-window modes.

responses.insight: Embedded tools risked increasing fragmentation.
responses.response: The center app browser became a transition strategy: older tools stayed accessible while applications were gradually migrated into native Conversant experiences.

responses.insight: Some problems should not become new standalone apps.
responses.response: In the digital fax workflow, ecosystem mapping showed that partnering with an existing file-processing tool would create a better workflow than building a separate new tool.

strategy.titleLead: One configurable workspace, not a collection of
intro: The strategic direction was to turn Conversant into a unified, configurable servicing workspace rather than a collection of disconnected tools. The platform needed to do three things at once.

The strongest design work was not only the design of individual features. It was the work of making those features behave coherently inside the larger Conversant environment, through reusable patterns, configurable views, persistent context, and clear interaction rules.

pillars.title: Provide orientation
pillars.desc: Representatives needed to know which member they were helping, what context mattered, what state they were in, and where they could act next.

pillars.title: Preserve flow
pillars.desc: Representatives needed to move between communication, member data, product information, notes, and task-specific apps without losing context.

pillars.title: Support variation
pillars.desc: Different employee groups needed different information and tools, but the platform still needed to feel coherent and learnable.

strategy.quoteLead: “The strongest work wasn't designing features — it was making them behave coherently inside

intro: Conversant was a system of interconnected workspaces, panels, controls, and embedded applications — each with a clear model for how representatives moved through it.

models.title: Desktop shell model
models.flow: Member tabs → Header → Left panel → Center workspace → Right panel → Comms
models.desc: The desktop shell gave employees a consistent structure for member context, servicing tools, communication, documentation, and task-specific applications.

models.title: Member context model
models.flow: Member tab → Header → Profile → Alerts → Contact info → Activity/history
models.desc: The platform kept member context visible and accessible so representatives could confirm who they were helping, understand the member's situation, and respond accurately during a live interaction.

models.title: Product relationship model
models.flow: Product lineup → Product details → Servicing/acquisition → Center app workflow
models.desc: The Product Panel helped representatives understand the member's relationship with USAA, review product lineup, and move into servicing or acquisition workflows.

models.title: Communication model
models.flow: Collapsed tool → Expanded tool → Caller controls → Agent state → Separate window
models.desc: Phone and chat tools needed to support live servicing without blocking critical information. The Phone Tool could be collapsed, expanded, or moved into a separate window when the expanded state interfered with the workspace.

models.title: Center app model
models.flow: Task-specific app → Native or web app → Shared container → Optional separate window
models.desc: The center workspace was the main launch point for internal applications. It supported both web apps and native Conversant apps, creating a migration path away from fragmented legacy tools.

models.title: Configuration model
models.flow: Manager settings → Role-specific views → Panel order, visibility, content
models.desc: The platform couldn't rely on one universal layout. Manager-controlled configuration tailored the experience to different teams while preserving a shared product structure.

models.title: Design-system model
models.flow: Native Conversant patterns + USAA web patterns → Platform-specific guidance
models.desc: Conversant had its own native desktop patterns while embedded web apps used the broader USAA design system — a dual environment where patterns had to be selected, adapted, or extended by context.

closing: Treating Conversant as a set of models rather than a fixed layout meant each workflow, role, and embedded app could plug into a shared structure that stayed coherent and learnable.

intro: Seven workstreams where I worked through the structure and detail of the platform — several of which could stand on their own as deeper case studies.

items.title: Product Panel redesign
desc: The Product Panel helped representatives understand the member's relationship with USAA, review product lineup, and move into servicing or acquisition workflows. Because different employee roles needed different product information and entry points, the panel had to balance consistency with configurability.

This work shows information architecture, role-specific servicing needs, manager-controlled configuration, and product navigation inside a dense enterprise workspace. It also shows how Conversant supported nonlinear workflows: sometimes the panel was only a reference point, while other times it became the launch point for a central servicing or acquisition app.

items.title: Phone Tool interactions
desc: The Phone Tool supported live member conversations inside Conversant. It included call controls, caller-specific actions, collapsed and expanded states, multiple-caller handling, hold and end-call behavior, wrap-up states, and separate-window behavior.

This work shows interaction design in a high-pressure workflow. Phone controls had to be clear, safe, and predictable because mistakes could directly affect a live member conversation. The team also designed around an AWS-based backend rather than assuming every ideal interaction was technically possible.

items.title: Phone & chat agent-state management
desc: Agent-state management needed to work across contexts. Representatives needed to see their current state, understand what state changes meant, and select a new state without confusion. This required a treatment that could work for both phone and chat while supporting different operational states.

items.title: Profile Alerts & Reason for Call notifications
desc: These features were less about adding UI and more about deciding where important context should live. They required strategic placement so representatives could see relevant information at the right moment without adding more noise to an already dense workspace.

items.title: Keyboard Shortcuts research & strategy
desc: Keyboard shortcuts mattered because Conversant was a repeated-use desktop tool. For experienced employees, keyboard support could reduce repetitive friction and help preserve speed. The work required understanding which shortcuts would actually support workflow efficiency instead of adding another layer to memorize.

items.title: Component Library
desc: Conversant needed reusable patterns for member tabs, the member header, side panels, side rails, and panel information sections. Component work helped the platform become more consistent and helped other teams design experiences that fit inside the larger Conversant environment.

items.title: Digital fax workflow strategy
desc: I led research and strategy for a digital fax tool integrated into the Conversant center app browser. The work included interviews, research synthesis, product requirement recommendations, and journey maps that showed each step in the workflow for different user types and tasks.

The journey maps extended beyond the fax tool itself, including the additional systems employees needed to complete the broader workflow — which revealed that building a new standalone tool would not solve the real complexity. After mapping the full ecosystem, I recommended partnering with the team that owned the main file-processing software and adding our requirements as new features within their existing tool.

That recommendation gave users a more streamlined workflow with less app switching and fewer repeated steps. It also helped USAA avoid the cost of standing up a new development team for a separate tool that would have added another application to an already complex ecosystem.

intro: Each decision balanced flexibility, density, safety, and buildability — the realities of a mature enterprise platform used all day, every day.

items.title: Design for a unified workspace, not a single linear flow
items.desc: Representatives needed to move between member context, communication, notes, product information, and task-specific tools in different orders depending on the situation.
items.tradeoff: Nonlinear workflows are harder to structure than step-by-step flows. The benefit was flexibility for real servicing work, where employees often respond to what happens during a live interaction.

items.title: Preserve persistent context
items.desc: Conversant kept member identity, profile details, panels, and communication tools available around the central workspace.
items.tradeoff: Persistent context increases interface density. The benefit was that representatives didn't have to leave a task-specific workflow to recover important member information.

items.title: Use configurability where one default couldn't serve everyone
items.desc: Different teams needed different information, tools, and panel setups.
items.tradeoff: Configurability can create complexity and governance overhead. The benefit was a platform that could support many roles without forcing every team into the same fixed layout.

items.title: Support veteran speed while improving learnability
items.desc: The platform needed to reduce reliance on workarounds without making experienced employees feel slower.
items.tradeoff: Designs that are immediately obvious aren't always fastest for expert repeated use. The goal was to design for the 50th use: patterns could be learned if they created long-term efficiency.

items.title: Design communication controls around operational safety
items.desc: Phone and chat interactions needed to be clear because they happened during live member interactions.
items.tradeoff: Richer controls can create visual complexity. The benefit was safer, more explicit handling of call states, multiple callers, wrap-up, hold, end-call behavior, and agent status.

items.title: Treat backend limitations as product constraints
items.desc: The Phone Tool had to work within the capabilities of an AWS-based backend.
items.tradeoff: Some ideal interactions weren't technically feasible. Designing inside real constraints let product, design, and engineering align on solutions that were usable and buildable.

items.title: Avoid creating a new tool when improving an existing workflow was better
items.desc: The digital fax research showed a standalone tool would add to ecosystem complexity rather than solve it.
items.tradeoff: Partnering with an existing product team reduces direct control over the final experience. The benefit was a simpler workflow for employees and better strategic use of development resources.

items.title: Balance native desktop patterns with embedded web-app patterns
items.desc: Conversant included native desktop experiences and web apps loaded inside the center browser.
items.tradeoff: Two design systems can create inconsistency if unmanaged. The benefit was using the right pattern set for each technical surface while establishing guidance for how experiences fit inside the platform.

items.title: Design for compliance without removing useful context
items.desc: Some user groups weren't allowed to see complete member information.
items.tradeoff: Redacted or simplified data views can reduce context. The benefit was a compliant experience that still gave employees enough information to complete their work accurately.

intro: Conversant was a mature, interconnected platform with many stakeholders. The constraints below shaped what was possible — and collaboration was how the work moved forward.

items.title: Existing platform structure
items.desc: When I joined, the basic version of the application had already been designed and developed. I worked within an existing app structure and an undocumented design system that was in use but not yet consistent.

items.title: Interconnected workflows
items.desc: Because the platform was complex and interconnected, each decision had to be evaluated against the rest of the application. A change that improved one workflow could affect another feature, role, or use case.

items.title: Diverse employee groups
items.desc: Conversant served many employee groups with nonlinear workflows. Different roles often had different priorities and different opinions about what information or controls mattered most.

items.title: Technical limitations
items.desc: Timeline pressure and existing infrastructure affected which designs could be accepted. Some stronger solutions required backup plans or phased improvements.

items.title: Backend constraints
items.desc: The Phone Tool had to work within the capabilities and limitations of an AWS-based backend. The design had to reflect what the system could support.

items.title: Compliance requirements
items.desc: Some users could not see complete member information. The design had to support simplified or redacted data views while still helping employees complete their work accurately.

items.title: Accessibility & keyboard support
items.desc: Conversant had to support long work sessions, high-frequency interactions, and keyboard-heavy workflows. Accessibility was not a layer added at the end — it was part of the quality bar for dense operational work.

collaboration: Conversant required collaboration across a large enterprise product ecosystem. I worked with the core Conversant design team, product teams, development teams, outside design teams building tools inside Conversant, MSR Labs for user testing coordination, accessibility partners, and design-system partners.

Because many internal tools were being migrated or built into Conversant, collaboration often meant more than handing off screens. It meant helping other teams understand employee workflows, adapt member-facing patterns for internal users, and align their work with the larger platform. I often served as an employee-user expert — helping teams design for USAA employees rather than external members, and ensuring their apps fit the workflows and usability needs of the internal servicing environment.

intro: When testing data was available, I evaluated design decisions through current-state versus redesigned usability scores, representative confidence, task clarity, and qualitative feedback. The goal was to connect results to specific design decisions rather than simply claim that a redesign "tested better."

For a complex enterprise platform, success was not only visual polish. It meant reducing confusion, improving confidence, supporting faster workflows, improving accessibility, and helping representatives complete complex servicing tasks with fewer unnecessary steps or workarounds.

questions.q: Can representatives find the information they need during a live servicing task?
Can new employees understand the workflow without relying entirely on memorized paths?
Can veteran employees preserve or improve speed?
Do role-specific views reduce noise without hiding essential context?
Do phone and chat controls clearly communicate state, actions, and consequences?
Do users understand alerts and notifications at the moment they appear?
Do keyboard shortcuts reduce repeated friction for high-frequency actions?
Do embedded apps feel like part of Conversant rather than another disconnected tool?
Can development partners build the solution within real backend and timeline constraints?

intro: Conversant improved the servicing environment by creating a more unified workspace for member context, communication controls, notes, product information, and task-specific tools. The platform reduced the need for representatives to move constantly between disconnected systems and gave internal teams a shared frame for building employee-facing experiences.

The strongest impact story is not that Conversant made one screen cleaner. The stronger story is that the work improved a complex internal product ecosystem by strengthening workflows, reducing fragmentation, creating reusable patterns, and helping teams make better product decisions inside real enterprise constraints.
highlights: A more unified servicing workspace for call center and back-office employees.
Reduced fragmentation across member data, product information, communication tools, and internal apps.
Clearer patterns for member tabs, member headers, panels, side rails, and information sections.
More flexible role-specific views through configuration and manager-controlled settings.
Improved support for nonlinear servicing workflows.
Improved research-driven decision-making for feature scope and direction.
Better alignment between design, product, development, accessibility, and business partners.
A migration path from fragmented legacy tools toward consistent Conversant-native experiences.
A strategic recommendation that avoided building a separate digital fax tool when improving an existing workflow was better.
demonstrates: Conversant demonstrates my ability to design inside complexity. The project required platform thinking, enterprise UX judgment, research-led product strategy, systems-level consistency, accessibility awareness, and practical collaboration across product, engineering, research, business, and design-system partners.

It also shows that impactful design is not always a blank-slate redesign. In mature enterprise systems, the most valuable work often comes from improving specific workflows, strengthening patterns, reducing inconsistency, and helping teams move faster without making the experience more fragmented.

The senior-level story is about judgment: knowing when to research, when to reuse patterns, when to create new component behavior, when to push for a stronger experience, when to accept implementation constraints, and when to recommend not building a new tool at all.

caseStudies.title: Product Panel Redesign
caseStudies.question: How might representatives quickly understand a member's product relationship and move into relevant servicing or acquisition workflows without overloading the workspace?

caseStudies.title: Phone Tool Interactions
caseStudies.question: How might phone controls support live conversations, multiple callers, agent states, wrap-up, and technical constraints without creating ambiguity?

caseStudies.title: Agent State Management
caseStudies.question: How might phone and chat states be represented consistently across contexts while helping representatives understand current state and next actions?

caseStudies.title: Profile Alerts & Reason for Call
caseStudies.question: How might important member context appear at the right moment without adding noise to an already dense workspace?

caseStudies.title: Keyboard Shortcuts Strategy
caseStudies.question: How might keyboard support improve speed for repeated-use expert workflows without adding unnecessary memorization burden?

caseStudies.title: Component Library
caseStudies.question: How might a mature internal platform become more consistent through shared patterns for tabs, headers, side panels, rails, and information sections?

caseStudies.title: Digital Fax Workflow Strategy
caseStudies.question: How might research reveal that the best solution is not a new product, but a better implementation strategy inside an existing workflow?

impact.confidentiality: Because Conversant was an internal enterprise platform, sensitive details, member data, operational workflows, and proprietary design decisions have been anonymized or recreated for public portfolio use.

hero.title: USAA Mobile
hero.subtitle: iOS, Android, and iPadOS redesign for USAA members

intro: USAA Mobile was a multi-year redesign of USAA's iOS, Android, and iPadOS apps: the primary digital access point for members to view accounts, manage policies, complete servicing actions, receive personalized reminders, and discover relevant products and services.

I worked on the redesign as a UX/UI Designer and Design Researcher over a three-year effort. My role spanned research synthesis, product strategy, information architecture, interaction design, visual design, native platform patterns, design-system contribution, and stakeholder communication.

Although this was a large enterprise product with many teams involved, I worked across several core parts of the redesign: the overall app organization, the Home model, product cards, public and private homepage experiences, customization, iPadOS adaptation, loading states, native design language exploration, and synthesis of member feedback.

challenge.titleLead: Help members act on a complex
challenge.hmwLead: How might we redesign a large, legacy financial app so members can quickly understand and act on their relationship with USAA —
challenge.hmwHighlight: while still supporting acquisition, personalization, servicing, platform conventions, and enterprise constraints?
intro: The challenge was not only to modernize the interface. The app had to help members move through a complex financial relationship with fewer unnecessary steps. A single member might have no products, a few products, or dozens of bank accounts, insurance policies, investments, external accounts, groups, hidden products, custom names, and balance-visibility preferences.

At the same time, USAA had business goals around product acquisition, personalized content, and cross-platform consistency. The redesign needed to reduce friction without turning Home into a generic dashboard that ignored business needs, marketing requirements, support paths, and future app experiences.
context: The USAA app had not been meaningfully redesigned since around 2007, even as member behavior was shifting toward mobile. App usage was rising quickly, app-only members were emerging, and the mobile experience was increasingly becoming the primary way members interacted with USAA.

The legacy experience had several problems. Navigation was difficult, interaction and performance felt slow, product and policy information was not always easy to access, and member data was underused. Product advertisements and advice could feel irrelevant, which trained members to ignore content that might have been useful if it had appeared with better timing and context.

The existing app was not all bad. Members valued quick access to common servicing actions, such as transferring funds. A critical part of the redesign was identifying what still worked and preserving that utility while rebuilding the structure around clearer member needs.

intro: The redesign was informed by app analytics, existing member feedback, usability testing, comparative testing, member interviews, competitive analysis, and heuristic review of the legacy app. The research and synthesis pointed to a few major patterns.

insights.title: Members wanted top-level access to what they owned
insights.desc: The strongest recurring need was simple: members wanted to see their accounts, policies, and products in one place without hunting through a confusing structure.

insights.title: Navigation friction created avoidable support needs
insights.desc: When members could not find basic information or common actions, they were more likely to rely on support channels for tasks the app should have made easier.

insights.title: Personalization had potential, but poor relevance created banner blindness
insights.desc: USAA had meaningful member context, but poorly timed or irrelevant marketing and advice reduced trust in the content area. The opportunity was to make personalized content feel more like service and less like interruption.

insights.title: Mobile and web needed a shared mental model
insights.desc: Members should not have to learn one structure on the website and another in the app. The redesign needed to align taxonomy, concepts, and member relationship models across platforms while still respecting native conventions.

insights.title: Member complexity needed flexible rules, not one-off screens
insights.desc: The app had to support members with very different product mixes: banking, insurance, investments, advice and services, external accounts, hidden accounts, groups, asset and liability totals, and custom organization preferences.

insights.title: Native design had to balance platform conventions with brand expression
insights.desc: The app needed to feel modern and native on iOS, Android, and iPadOS, while still expressing USAA's brand and contributing to a broader native design language.

responses.insight: Members wanted to see everything they owned in one place.
responses.requirement: Make the member relationship visible at the top level.
responses.response: A Home model centered on product cards for Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services.

responses.insight: Navigation confusion increased friction and avoidable support needs.
responses.requirement: Simplify the first-level structure and create clear routes for known tasks and help-seeking moments.
responses.response: A simplified tab model around Home, Search, and Chat, with Home acting as the center of gravity.

responses.insight: The app needed to support both servicing and acquisition.
responses.requirement: Give members immediate access to owned products and actions while creating structured spaces for relevant product discovery.
responses.response: Product cards that combined accounts, policies, servicing actions, product suggestions, and acquisition entry points.

responses.insight: Personalized content was easy to ignore when it felt generic.
responses.requirement: Make reminders, advice, and marketing more contextual.
responses.response: Activity cards for relevant moments such as upcoming bills, active claims, travel notices, and other member-specific prompts.

responses.insight: Member product relationships were highly variable.
responses.requirement: Create flexible rules for long product lists, custom names, groups, hidden products, and balance visibility.
responses.response: A product-card system paired with a single Customize experience for renaming, hiding, grouping, and reordering.

responses.insight: Tablet behavior could not simply stretch the phone app.
responses.requirement: Adapt the system to iPadOS interaction patterns.
responses.response: A split-view structure, persistent fullscreen behavior, platform-specific modal patterns, and simple column-width rules.

intro: The strategic direction was to make Home the member's relationship hub: a place where members could see what they owned, act on common needs, discover relevant next steps, and receive timely reminders.

Home was not treated as a marketing surface, a product list, or a generic dashboard alone. It had to be all of these in the right balance. The redesign organized Home around owned products first, then layered in actions, acquisition, activity cards, reminders, and support paths.

This strategy also let the app align with the redesigned Member Home Web experience. The goal was not to make mobile and web identical, but to make them conceptually familiar — the same member relationship model across platforms, while native app patterns shaped the interaction details.
strategy.quoteLead: “Home had to be a product list, an action center, a personalization surface, and an acquisition space —

intro: The redesigned app was not one screen. It was a system of related flows that had to support different member needs while staying understandable at the first level.

models.title: Core app structure
models.flow: Home → Search → Chat
models.desc: A simple first-level organization. Home supported the member relationship and contextual content. Search supported direct navigation when members knew what they wanted. Chat provided a persistent support path for help-oriented moments.

models.title: Member relationship model
models.flow: Home → Product card → Account or policy → Summary page
models.desc: Home organized member-owned products into cards for Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services. Tapping an account or policy led to a summary experience, creating a clear path from top-level overview to product detail.

models.title: Servicing model
models.flow: Product card or summary → Action → Modal sheet or destination
models.desc: Common actions such as Transfer, Pay Bills, Deposit, and Send were surfaced near the related product context. Many focused servicing actions used modal sheets to preserve a lightweight native feel while keeping members oriented.

models.title: Acquisition & connection model
models.flow: Product card → Add account / Get quote → Menu or modal → Product path
models.desc: The same product-card structure gave USAA a place for acquisition and external account connection without forcing generic marketing into unrelated areas. The challenge was making product discovery feel contextual rather than intrusive.

models.title: Personalized activity model
models.flow: Member context → Activity card → Reminder, task, or next step
models.desc: Activity cards created a place for personalized content such as upcoming bills, active claims, and travel notices. These cards helped shift personalization from generic promotion toward timely assistance.

models.title: Customization model
models.flow: Home → Edit → Customize → Rename, hide, group, reorder → Done
models.desc: Customization translated existing web and legacy-app functionality into modern native patterns. Members could rename accounts or policies, hide products or balances, create groups, reorder products, move items between groups, and rearrange entire sections.

models.title: iPadOS model
models.flow: Home split view → Summary → Fullscreen toggle / back navigation
models.desc: The iPadOS adaptation created a structure that could support Home and detail views at the same level. A persistent fullscreen toggle solved a legacy interaction problem by letting members move in and out of fullscreen even after navigating deeper.

closing: The system needed to preserve simplicity at the first level while still scaling to a large number of products, actions, account states, support paths, acquisition needs, and platform-specific interaction patterns.

intro: The strongest solution was rarely the cleanest screen or the most minimal first level. Each decision balanced member expectations, business goals, existing behaviors, and platform constraints.

items.title: Make Home the center of the app
items.desc: Home became the primary place where members could understand their relationship with USAA — combining owned products, common actions, activity cards, acquisition paths, and personalized content.
items.tradeoff: Home risked becoming overloaded. The answer wasn't to remove complexity but to organize it into clearer zones and cards members could parse quickly.

items.title: Show member-owned products at the top level
items.desc: The team explored showing only a selection of accounts, placing the rest on a deeper page, adding expand/collapse, and showing all products by default.
items.tradeoff: Showing everything could make Home longer for members with many products. Research and business reasoning supported full visibility: members preferred seeing all products in one place, and most had relatively few accounts.

items.title: Use product cards as a flexible container
items.desc: Product cards let Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services share a common model while supporting different content types, actions, states, and suggestions.
items.tradeoff: A shared card model had to avoid becoming too generic — enough consistency to feel predictable, enough flexibility to support very different product categories.

items.title: Balance servicing and acquisition
items.desc: Members came to complete tasks and understand their accounts, but the business also needed to support product discovery and engagement.
items.tradeoff: Too much marketing would create banner blindness; too little would weaken the business value. The answer was acquisition entry points in product-relevant contexts and personalized content that felt timely.

items.title: Use modal sheets for focused native tasks
items.desc: Servicing actions and acquisition menus often fit modal sheets well because they were focused, temporary, and task-oriented.
items.tradeoff: Not every experience belonged in a modal. Complex areas like Profile were better suited to navigation views — requiring a view-type strategy rather than one pattern.

items.title: Create one Customize experience, not many editors
items.desc: Customization involved many related actions: rename, hide, hide balances, reorder, group, ungroup, and move items between sections. A single Customize space unified them.
items.tradeoff: One shared experience increased the need for clear structure and rules, but let members manage their full relationship in one place rather than learning separate editing patterns per card.

items.title: Respect native conventions while shaping a new design language
items.desc: The app needed to feel native on iOS, Android, and iPadOS while USAA evolved its broader visual and interaction language.
items.tradeoff: Over-customization could feel less native or harder to build; over-reliance on defaults could weaken brand and consistency. The redesign defined a middle path: native behavior with a recognizable USAA system.

items.title: Treat iPadOS as an adaptation, not a resize
items.desc: The iPad version needed a structure that made better use of screen space while supporting Home, summaries, Search, Chat, Profile, transfer, and other flows.
items.tradeoff: The team explored custom patterns but leaned into familiar iPad conventions like split view and SwiftUI-style modals — easier to understand, scale, and build.

intro: The project required collaboration across multiple design teams, business partners, developers, and executives. My contribution spanned research framing through native execution.

blocks.title: Research synthesis & problem framing
blocks.desc: I helped synthesize years of member feedback, app analytics, usability findings, competitive patterns, and legacy-app pain points into a clearer direction: members needed faster access to owned products, better servicing paths, and more useful personalized content.

blocks.title: Information architecture & app structure
blocks.desc: I contributed to the app's overall organization, including the Home, Search, and Chat model; destination patterns for account and policy summaries; and the role of modal sheets versus navigation views.

blocks.title: Home & product-card design
blocks.desc: I worked on the Home model and product-card system, including how Banking, Insurance, Investing, and Advice & Services could organize owned products, common actions, product suggestions, and contextual member content.

blocks.title: Customization flows
blocks.desc: I worked through how existing customization functionality could translate into a modern native experience — entry points, view types, renaming, hiding products and balances, grouping, reordering, and returning members to Home.

blocks.title: iPadOS adaptation
blocks.desc: I contributed to the tablet adaptation, including split-view behavior, fullscreen interactions, modal decisions, and layout rules that could scale across iPad sizes.

blocks.title: Design-system & native language contribution
blocks.desc: The redesign helped shape USAA's native design language. In many cases we weren't simply applying finished components — we were defining patterns and components other teams could later reuse.

blocks.title: Stakeholder communication
blocks.desc: A major part of the work was explaining tradeoffs, aligning teams around member evidence, and balancing competing priorities without losing the core product direction.

intro: The redesign was supported by both existing evidence and validation during the design process. Key signals included member preference for seeing all products in one place, reduced navigation confusion in comparative testing, and clearer understanding of the redesigned Home structure.
For a public portfolio version, exact performance numbers remain confidential, but the measurement story can still be framed around the right success signals.

questions.q: Can members find accounts and policies faster?
Can members complete common servicing tasks with fewer steps?
Does the redesigned Home reduce navigation confusion?
Do members use Search and Chat as intentional support paths rather than recovery from poor navigation?
Does contextual content perform better than generic promotional content?
Do product-card acquisition paths drive engagement without increasing banner blindness?
Do members understand and use customization features?
Does the iPadOS structure improve navigation and multitasking versus the legacy model?
Can the design system support other teams more efficiently after the redesign?

intro: The final shipped app reflected several of the project's major design decisions, including the Home structure, product-card model, customization patterns, native interaction direction, and iPadOS adaptation.

The work also influenced USAA beyond the mobile app itself. The redesigned app helped establish patterns for USAA's broader native design language, and many components and interaction models contributed to the design-system overhaul.

The strongest story is not simply that the app was redesigned. It's that the work shows how to simplify a large consumer-facing product while respecting business complexity, technical constraints, member evidence, platform conventions, and enterprise collaboration.
demonstrates: USAA Mobile demonstrates my ability to work inside a large, constrained, compliance-heavy enterprise environment while still thinking like a product designer. The work required connecting research, IA, interaction design, platform strategy, design systems, business priorities, and stakeholder alignment.

It also shows disciplined product judgment. The strongest solution was the structure that matched member expectations, supported business goals, preserved valuable existing behaviors, scaled across platforms, and gave future teams a reusable foundation.

caseStudies.title: Home & Product-Card Model
caseStudies.question: How might Home organize owned products, actions, acquisition, activity cards, and personalization without becoming overwhelming?

caseStudies.title: Equal Access vs. Prioritization
caseStudies.question: How might the team decide whether to show all member products at the first level or prioritize only a subset?

caseStudies.title: Organization & Interaction Model
caseStudies.question: How might Home, Search, Chat, account summaries, servicing actions, acquisition menus, Profile, and modal sheets fit into one app structure?

caseStudies.title: Customization
caseStudies.question: How might customization from Member Home Web and the legacy app translate into a modern native experience?

caseStudies.title: iPadOS Adaptation
caseStudies.question: How might a phone-first financial app become a tablet-optimized system with split view, fullscreen behavior, modal patterns, and simple layout rules?

caseStudies.title: Design-System Contribution
caseStudies.question: How might a major app redesign help define reusable native patterns for the broader organization?

impact.confidentiality: Because this work involved USAA's internal product strategy, member data, and proprietary design decisions, sensitive details and visuals have been anonymized or recreated for public portfolio use.

hero.title: Member Home
hero.subtitle: Authenticated homepage redesign for USAA members

intro: Member Home was USAA's authenticated homepage: the first page members saw after signing in to view accounts, manage policies, complete common servicing actions, track activity, and discover relevant products and services.

I contributed to the redesign as a UX/UI Designer, working across research synthesis, product strategy, information architecture, interaction design, visual design, design-system contribution, implementation detail, and stakeholder collaboration. The work transformed a cluttered legacy homepage into a scalable product system built around what members own, what needs attention, and what they can do next.

This was not only a visual refresh. Member Home had accumulated years of business requests, product-line complexity, legacy interaction patterns, and underperforming content. The redesign required a stronger page strategy, clearer governance, reusable component behavior, and a more disciplined way to balance member needs with business priorities.

stats.label: members in early rollout (≈ half of USAA)

challenge.titleLead: A high-traffic homepage that couldn't become a
challenge.hmwLead: How might we redesign a high-traffic authenticated homepage so members can quickly understand their relationship with USAA, act on timely needs, and find relevant products —
challenge.hmwHighlight: without letting the page become a dumping ground for every business priority?
intro: The previous Member Home mixed accounts, policies, marketing, servicing actions, status updates, and legacy content without a strong organizing model. Members had to scan unrelated information to understand what they owned, what needed attention, and where they could act.

The deeper challenge was governance. A homepage like this carries pressure from every line of business — each team has something to promote, surface, or maintain. The redesign needed a decision system for what belonged on Member Home, what should move deeper, and how the page could stay useful over time.
context: Member Home had not meaningfully evolved in years. It was one of USAA's most important authenticated surfaces, but it was weighed down by difficult navigation, irrelevant information, cluttered marketing, low content engagement, limited responsive behavior, and slow technical performance.

The page also had to support many member situations at once: checking balances, viewing insurance policies, paying bills, transferring funds, reviewing claims, continuing applications, managing external accounts, finding products, and customizing account visibility.

The redesign had to preserve what was useful while removing or restructuring what created noise. The work was not about simplifying by stripping everything away — it was about simplifying with purpose: keeping what helped members understand, decide, or act.

intro: The team started by understanding the existing page: what members used, what they ignored, where they struggled, and what business partners needed it to support. Research and synthesis spanned member feedback, usage data, stakeholder input, IA exploration, co-design interviews, qualitative and quantitative testing, and analysis of current page elements.

insights.title: Members needed a clearer way to understand their relationship with USAA
insights.desc: The page had to make owned accounts, policies, and products easier to scan, organize, and act on.

insights.title: The page had too much undifferentiated content
insights.desc: Many elements competed for attention without a clear hierarchy. Some had low utilization or negative feedback — a sign of poor implementation or limited underlying value.

insights.title: Business priorities needed structure, not unlimited space
insights.desc: Marketing and acquisition couldn't simply be removed, but they needed to appear in ways that felt relevant, contextual, and respectful of the member's task.

insights.title: Personalization had to feel helpful, not intrusive
insights.desc: USAA could surface timely content, but the value depended on relevance, timing, and clarity.

insights.title: The experience needed a durable organizing model
insights.desc: The redesign couldn't depend on one-off layout decisions. It needed a system that could scale across products, activity, marketing, servicing actions, responsive breakpoints, and future content requests.

insights.title: Design-system constraints were part of the product problem
insights.desc: The design language system was evolving, so the team worked with existing components while extending or creating new component behavior where the experience required it.

responses.insight: Members needed to quickly understand what they owned.
responses.requirement: Make the member's relationship with USAA visible and scannable.
responses.response: A Products section organized around durable categories: Banking, Insurance, Investments, and Advice & Services.

responses.insight: The homepage was overloaded with competing priorities.
responses.requirement: Create a clear content model and governance standard.
responses.response: A decision framework around whether content helped members understand, decide, or act.

responses.insight: Timely tasks and updates were buried or scattered.
responses.requirement: Surface relevant activity at the moment it mattered.
responses.response: A prioritized Activity feed for claims updates, bill reminders, recent transactions, credit score content, tax reminders, and other contextual prompts.

responses.insight: Servicing actions needed to be easier to find but not generic.
responses.requirement: Show actions only when relevant to the member's products.
responses.response: Contextual servicing actions within product categories, such as Transfer, Pay Bills, Auto Proof, Claims, Auto Invest, and Buy/Sell.

responses.insight: Product acquisition needed to support the business without feeling intrusive.
responses.requirement: Integrate acquisition into the member's existing relationship context.
responses.response: Contextual product acquisition inside product categories, replacing broad mega-menu behavior with focused entry points.

responses.insight: Marketing was being ignored when it felt irrelevant.
responses.requirement: Make marketing fewer, more relevant, and visually connected to the product relationship.
responses.response: Product-adjacent marketing cards designed to feel like they could become owned products.

responses.insight: Existing customization features had value but were difficult to use.
responses.requirement: Preserve member control while simplifying the interaction model.
responses.response: A redesigned Customize mode for reordering, hiding, grouping, renaming, switching views, and temporarily showing hidden accounts or policies.

responses.insight: External account aggregation created confusing states.
responses.requirement: Clarify third-party error, sign-in, and loading scenarios.
responses.response: Fifteen inherited scenarios organized into nine logical groups with clearer messages, treatments, and resolution paths.

intro: The strategic direction was to turn Member Home from a cluttered landing page into a relationship hub, organized around a simple mental model.

The Products section helped members understand and manage their relationship with USAA. The Activity feed surfaced timely information and next steps. Contextual actions and acquisition paths helped members act without turning the page into a generic shortcut directory or a marketing page.

The strategy also created a governance principle: new content or capabilities had to earn their place by helping members understand, decide, or act. That standard mattered because the long-term risk wasn't only the old page's clutter — it was the chance the redesigned page would slowly become cluttered again.

pillars.title: Products
pillars.desc: What members own — accounts, policies, and products, grouped so they're easy to scan and manage.

pillars.title: Activity
pillars.desc: What needs attention — reminders, statuses, and timely next steps surfaced by priority.

pillars.title: Actions
pillars.desc: What members can do next — contextual servicing and acquisition tied to the products they hold.

strategy.quoteLead: “New content had to earn its place by helping members

intro: Member Home was designed as a system of related models rather than a single static page — so it could scale across products, activity, marketing, servicing, responsive breakpoints, and future content requests while preserving simplicity.

models.title: Top-level page model
models.flow: Products + Activity
models.desc: The page split into two high-level sections. Products focused on what the member owned and could manage; Activity focused on what needed attention — reminders, updates, statuses, and timely next steps.

models.title: Product relationship model
models.flow: Product category → Account or policy → Data, actions, messages
models.desc: The Products section grouped accounts and policies into Banking, Insurance, Investments, and Advice & Services — helping members scan the page and giving teams a consistent structure for owned products, actions, marketing, and messaging.

models.title: Account & policy card model
models.flow: Owned product → Key data → Status / message → Action
models.desc: Cards had to show required data without overwhelming members across different product types, relationships, and account counts. Grid view offered modern scannability; list view offered a traditional tally with asset and liability totals.

models.title: Contextual servicing model
models.flow: Product context → Relevant action → Destination owned by another team
models.desc: Servicing actions appeared only when relevant. Pay Bills wouldn't show for a member with only a savings account in Banking, because savings accounts don't generate bills — keeping the page useful instead of a generic shortcut list.

models.title: Contextual acquisition model
models.flow: Product category → Focused acquisition menu → Product page or service
models.desc: Acquisition was integrated into the same category structure as owned products. Instead of a broad top-nav mega menu, members explored relevant products from the category where they made sense.

models.title: Product lifecycle model
models.flow: Marketing → In-progress application → Owned product
models.desc: The team coined a product lifecycle: a product could begin as marketing, become an in-progress application, and eventually an owned product — connecting acquisition to the member relationship rather than treating marketing as separate.

models.title: Activity feed model
