models.flow: Member context → Prioritized card → Reminder, status, shortcut, next step
models.desc: The Activity feed was a prioritization system, not a static widget column. It ranked cards by member utilization, product lineup, and events like tax season, giving product teams a framework to surface useful content without equal prominence.

models.title: Customization model
models.flow: Customize → Rename, hide, group, reorder, switch view → Return
models.desc: Customize mode preserved member control over organization: reordering, hiding, grouping, renaming, switching between grid and list view, and temporarily showing hidden products outside customize mode.

models.title: External account state model
models.flow: Third-party state → Clear message → Resolution path
models.desc: External aggregation introduced confusing states because USAA relied on a third party to pull outside data. The redesign grouped inherited error, sign-in, and loading scenarios into clearer categories with resolution steps.

closing: Treating the page as a set of models rather than a fixed layout meant each product team could plug into a shared structure — and the page could grow without sliding back into clutter.

intro: The best solution was rarely the page with the fewest elements. Each decision balanced member needs, business goals, personalization, component constraints, and long-term governance.

items.title: Divide the page into Products and Activity
items.desc: A two-part structure separated what members owned from what needed attention.
items.tradeoff: A more structured page risks hiding cross-cutting content that could belong in multiple places. The benefit was a clearer mental model: look left for your relationship, right for timely next steps.

items.title: Use durable product categories
items.desc: Banking, Insurance, Investments, and Advice & Services became the permanent categories. IA testing showed members resonated with these labels.
items.tradeoff: Permanent categories create consistency but require many product teams to fit a shared model. The benefit was stronger scanning and a foundation for contextual actions and acquisition.

items.title: Support both grid and list views
items.desc: Members could view accounts and policies in grid (modern, scannable) or list (traditional tally with asset and liability totals).
items.tradeoff: Two views increased design and implementation complexity. The benefit was flexibility for different member mental models and account-management habits.

items.title: Make actions contextual
items.desc: Rather than every possible shortcut, the page surfaced servicing actions only when corresponding accounts or policies were present.
items.tradeoff: Contextual actions require rules and coordination across product teams. The benefit was a cleaner page where actions felt relevant instead of generic.

items.title: Integrate acquisition into the product relationship
items.desc: Acquisition moved from broad mega-menu behavior into focused, category-based entry points.
items.tradeoff: Acquisition had to be visible enough for business goals without disrupting servicing and account-management tasks. Placing it near relevant categories kept it contextual.

items.title: Redesign marketing as product-adjacent
items.desc: Marketing became a variant of the product component so it felt related to the member's financial relationship rather than a separate banner.
items.tradeoff: Marketing needed to perform without taking over the page. The theory — fewer, more relevant placements reduce banner blindness — held: early rollout showed 4.3× click-through and 2.4× quote starts.

items.title: Create a reusable activity-card framework
items.desc: The Activity feed gave many product teams a shared framework for timely, personalized content.
items.tradeoff: A feed can become noisy if everything is equally important. The prioritization model preserved usefulness by ranking content by member context, product lineup, utilization, and seasonal events.

items.title: Preserve customization while redesigning the interactions
items.desc: The existing customization was rooted in real needs but confusing to use; the redesign kept the capabilities and rebuilt the interaction model.
items.tradeoff: Customization adds complexity to the page system. The benefit was member control: organizing, hiding, renaming, grouping, and viewing products to match a personal mental model.

items.title: Simplify external account states
items.desc: Third-party aggregation introduced multiple inherited error, sign-in, and loading scenarios.
items.tradeoff: Detailed messages take more design and content work than a generic error. The benefit was clarity: members could understand exactly what was happening and what to do next.

intro: The page touched many teams and business priorities, so collaboration was essential. My contribution ran from research synthesis through interaction detail, iconography, and developer-ready specifications.

blocks.title: Research synthesis & content audit
blocks.desc: I helped analyze the existing page, member feedback, usage patterns, stakeholder needs, and the value of current page elements — identifying what should remain, move elsewhere, or be removed.

blocks.title: Information architecture & category model
blocks.desc: I contributed to the category model organizing accounts and policies into Banking, Insurance, Investments, and Advice & Services, supporting owned products, servicing actions, acquisition, and marketing.

blocks.title: Account & policy card design
blocks.desc: I worked on card hierarchy explorations — how required data should be represented across grid and list views while keeping the experience scannable.

blocks.title: Activity card design
blocks.desc: I designed activity-card concepts including the Claims card and Real Estate Rewards Network card, requiring service-journey thinking, status design, prioritization, and constrained implementation decisions.

blocks.title: Customize mode interactions
blocks.desc: I contributed to the redesign of customize mode: entry and exit points, transitions, drag-and-drop reordering, lightweight popovers, hiding and renaming, grouping, deleting groups, switching views, and temporarily showing hidden accounts.

blocks.title: Design-system & implementation details
blocks.desc: I worked with evolving design-system components while developing new component behavior — interaction details, developer-ready specifications, responsive behavior, and iconography.

blocks.title: Custom icon design
blocks.desc: I created custom icons for Insurance Bill, Auto Invest, and Buy/Sell. They aligned to the existing icon library and were used on the page and elsewhere in the app and website.

blocks.title: Stakeholder collaboration
blocks.desc: The work required translating many teams' stakeholder needs into a coherent member-centered system rather than giving every request equal visual priority.

intro: The redesign was shaped by qualitative and quantitative testing of common tasks, secondary tasks, and acquisition paths. The team also used usage data and member feedback to evaluate current-state problems and remove low-value or low-use content.

Validation focused on whether members could better understand the page structure, locate products, complete common actions, engage with relevant content, and respond to acquisition without feeling overwhelmed by marketing. The clearest measurable impact came from the marketing redesign: after rollout to roughly half of membership — about 6 million people — the experience saw a 4.3× increase in marketing click-through and a 2.4× increase in quote starts.

questions.q: Can members find owned accounts and policies more easily?
Can members distinguish what they own from what needs attention?
Are contextual actions easier to find and use?
Does relevant marketing perform better than broad promotional placement?
Does the Activity feed create a scalable framework for timely member content?
Do members understand external account states and resolution paths?
Can business teams add content without undermining page simplicity?
Can the component system support responsive layouts and future feature growth?

intro: The redesign created a clearer and more purposeful Member Home experience. It clarified the relationship between products and activity, reduced unnecessary clutter, made servicing actions more contextual, improved marketing performance, and created a more scalable framework for future content.

The strongest story is not only that the page became cleaner. It's that the redesign created a sustainable product system for a complex enterprise homepage — one that balanced member needs, business goals, personalization, component constraints, and long-term governance.
highlights: Rolled out to roughly half of USAA membership — about 6 million people.
Clearer governance for what belongs on Member Home.
Stronger structure for product teams to add content without undermining simplicity.
Reusable component behavior for product cards, activity cards, contextual messages, and responsive layouts.
demonstrates: Member Home demonstrates my ability to work on complex enterprise product systems where UX, business goals, design systems, and governance all intersect. The work required product judgment: deciding what belongs on a critical homepage, how to organize competing priorities, how to make personalization useful, and how to preserve simplicity over time.

It also shows senior-level design thinking through tradeoffs. The best solution was not the page with the fewest elements — it was the system that made the right elements easier to understand, act on, govern, and scale.

caseStudies.title: Products + Activity System
caseStudies.question: How might a complex authenticated homepage organize what members own, what needs attention, and what they can do next?

caseStudies.title: Account & Policy Card System
caseStudies.question: How might cards display required information while staying scannable across different product types and member relationships?

caseStudies.title: Personalized Marketing
caseStudies.question: How might marketing become fewer, more relevant, and more product-adjacent while improving business performance?

caseStudies.title: Activity Feed Framework
caseStudies.question: How might a prioritized feed surface claims updates, bill reminders, tax prompts, transaction activity, and other timely content?

caseStudies.title: Claims Activity Card
caseStudies.question: How might a card help members understand claim status, enter the claims center, and track messages from claims adjusters?

caseStudies.title: Real Estate Rewards Network Card
caseStudies.question: How might a larger home-buying reward journey be simplified into a constrained activity card while preserving member value?

caseStudies.title: External Account States
caseStudies.question: How might inherited third-party aggregation errors become clear, actionable messages with resolution paths?

caseStudies.title: Customize Mode
caseStudies.question: How might members reorder, hide, group, rename, and switch views without turning customization into a confusing separate product?

caseStudies.title: Design-System Contribution
caseStudies.question: How might a major homepage redesign extend component behavior, responsive rules, iconography, and developer-ready specs?

impact.confidentiality: Because this work involved USAA's internal product strategy, member data, proprietary design decisions, and business performance, sensitive details and visuals have been anonymized or recreated for public portfolio use.
